title: Street Pavement Deterioration Across El Paso
statement: Only 31% of El Paso residents report satisfaction with street conditions. The city maintains over 6,500 lane-miles of roadway with a backlog of deferred maintenance exceeding $900M. Priority corridors in Districts 3, 5, and 8 require full reconstruction.
evidence: FY27-28 Strategic Plan: 31% resident satisfaction; Community Survey 2025
estimatedScale: $900M+ deferred maintenance backlog
planSource: FY2027-FY2028 CoEP Strategic Plan — Infrastructure Pillar

title: Living-Wage Job Deficit and Workforce Misalignment
statement: Only 22% of El Paso residents express satisfaction with availability of living-wage jobs. Workforce pathways are misaligned with employer needs in healthcare, advanced manufacturing, logistics, and defense sectors. Barriers include childcare access and skills training gaps.
evidence: FY27-28 Strategic Plan: 22% satisfaction; 47% among younger residents; employer surveys
estimatedScale: $50M-$100M in regional workforce investment needed
planSource: FY2027-FY2028 CoEP Strategic Plan — Economic Mobility Pillar

title: Rural Water Infrastructure Failure in Eastern El Paso County
statement: Unincorporated communities in eastern El Paso County (Tornillo, Fabens, Clint) operate aging water treatment systems with 40%+ system loss rates. Approximately 12,000 residents rely on infrastructure that fails to meet EPA standards. No capital improvement plans exist.
evidence: TCEQ compliance reports; El Paso Water Utilities gap analysis
estimatedScale: $75M-$120M for system replacement and expansion
planSource: Regional Research Collaborative Strategy — Tier 1 Priority

title: Border Freight Congestion and Cross-Border Mobility
statement: El Paso's 4 international bridges process $100B+ in annual trade but experience 2-4 hour commercial crossing delays. Freight congestion costs the regional economy an estimated $1.2B annually. Smart infrastructure and port-of-entry modernization are critical to supply chain competitiveness.
evidence: Borderplex Alliance trade data; USDOT Border Crossing statistics
estimatedScale: $1.2B annual economic cost; $500M+ infrastructure investment needed
planSource: Federal Opportunity Intelligence System — Signal: DOT Smart Freight

title: First Responder Recruitment and Retention Crisis
statement: El Paso PD and Fire Department face 15-20% vacancy rates. Competitive regional salaries and burnout drive attrition. Community safety programs require data-driven resource allocation and mental health crisis intervention capacity.
evidence: FY27-28 Strategic Plan: 63% feel safe (above national avg); vacancy reports
estimatedScale: $25M-$40M annually in recruitment, retention, and program costs
planSource: FY2027-FY2028 CoEP Strategic Plan — Public Safety Pillar

title: Housing Affordability and Supply Shortage
statement: El Paso's housing stock is misaligned with demand. Median household income of $49,000 constrains homeownership. Infill development is underutilized while sprawl strains infrastructure. Mixed-income housing along transit corridors is a priority in both El Paso and Las Cruces.
evidence: ACS housing data; Strategic Plan community survey; Las Cruces El Paseo Corridor Plan
estimatedScale: $200M+ in housing development and subsidy needed over 5 years
planSource: CoEP Strategic Plan — Economic Mobility; Las Cruces Strategic Plan — Community Prosperity

title: Agricultural Water Stress and Irrigation Modernization
statement: The Paso del Norte agricultural economy depends on Rio Grande allocations that have declined 30% over the past decade. Flood irrigation methods waste 50-60% of allocated water. Pecan orchards, cotton, and chile producers in Doña Ana County and eastern El Paso County face existential risk.
evidence: EBID allocation records; USDA crop reports; Rio Grande Compact monitoring
estimatedScale: $150M+ for irrigation modernization and water recycling systems
planSource: Regional Research Collaborative Strategy — Cross-jurisdictional Priority

title: Las Cruces Renewable Energy Transition and Grid Resilience
statement: Las Cruces adopted a Climate Action Plan in 2020 targeting carbon neutrality. The city's grid infrastructure cannot support distributed solar at scale. Grid resilience planning is critical given extreme heat events exceeding 100°F for 70+ days annually.
evidence: Las Cruces Climate Action Plan 2020; NOAA extreme heat data
estimatedScale: $100M-$200M in grid modernization and renewable deployment
planSource: Las Cruces Strategic Plan 2021-2026 — Environmental Sustainability

title: Public Health Workforce and Behavioral Health Access Gap
statement: The Borderplex region is designated a Health Professional Shortage Area (HPSA) for mental health. El Paso County has 1 mental health provider per 500+ residents. Las Cruces and El Paso both prioritize behavioral health integration, crisis intervention, and community health worker pipelines.
evidence: HRSA HPSA designation; DSHS provider ratios; Strategic Plans from both cities
estimatedScale: $60M-$100M in workforce training and facility expansion
planSource: CoEP Strategic Plan — Quality of Life; Las Cruces — Education & Community Services

title: Cross-Border Air Quality Degradation
statement: The El Paso-Juárez airshed consistently exceeds EPA ozone and PM2.5 standards. Brick kilns, industrial emissions from maquiladoras, and vehicle congestion at ports of entry contribute to respiratory illness rates 20% above national average. Binational monitoring is fragmented.
evidence: TCEQ monitoring data; EPA Border 2025 reports; WHO air quality guidelines
estimatedScale: $50M+ for monitoring, mitigation, and binational coordination
planSource: Regional Research Collaborative Strategy — Binational Priority

title: Multimodal Transportation Connectivity Gaps
statement: El Paso and Las Cruces lack integrated transit connecting employment centers, educational institutions, and residential areas. Sun Metro ridership has declined 15% since 2019. No regional transit authority spans the TX/NM border. Safe pedestrian and cycling infrastructure is underdeveloped.
evidence: Sun Metro ridership data; MPO long-range transportation plan; community surveys
estimatedScale: $300M+ for regional transit and active transportation network
planSource: CoEP Strategic Plan — Infrastructure; Las Cruces — Capital Improvement

title: Socorro Rapid Growth Without Infrastructure
statement: Socorro's population has grown 25% in a decade while water/wastewater capacity remains flat. The city has no dedicated economic development office. Commercial tax base is minimal, limiting public safety and infrastructure investment.
evidence: Census population estimates; TCEQ water system capacity reports
estimatedScale: $40M-$60M for water, roads, and public safety facilities
planSource: Regional Plans Analysis — LGI Layer

title: Defense Sector Workforce Pipeline Gap
statement: Fort Bliss, White Sands Missile Range, and 15+ defense contractors in the region report difficulty filling cybersecurity, systems engineering, and cleared technical positions. UTEP graduates 200+ engineers annually but retention in the region is below 40%.
evidence: Fort Bliss workforce reports; defense contractor hiring data; UTEP career services
estimatedScale: $30M-$50M in training, clearance pipelines, and retention incentives
planSource: UTEP-NM Funding Partner Mapping — Defense Sector

title: Broadband Desert in Rural Colonias
statement: Approximately 35% of households in eastern El Paso County and southern Doña Ana County lack broadband access (25 Mbps+). Colonias and CDPs are systematically underserved by commercial ISPs. Remote work, telehealth, and distance education are inaccessible.
evidence: FCC broadband maps; ACS internet access data; NTIA indicators
estimatedScale: $80M-$150M for fiber deployment and last-mile connectivity
planSource: Regional Research Collaborative Strategy — Infrastructure Priority

title: Manufacturing Supply Chain Optimization
statement: Ciudad Juárez hosts 330+ maquiladoras but supply chain disruptions (COVID, border delays, semiconductor shortages) cost the binational manufacturing ecosystem an estimated $2B since 2020. Advanced manufacturing, reshoring incentives, and cross-border logistics optimization are urgent.
evidence: INDEX Juárez reports; USMCA trade data; Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas analysis
estimatedScale: $2B+ in cumulative disruption costs since 2020
planSource: UTEP Partner Pipeline — Advanced Manufacturing Sector

title: Santa Teresa Port of Entry Capacity and Industrial Development
statement: Santa Teresa is the fastest-growing commercial port on the US-Mexico border but lacks adequate road infrastructure, rail intermodal capacity, and workforce housing. Industrial development has outpaced infrastructure planning.
evidence: CBP port statistics; NM Border Authority reports; MVEDA development data
estimatedScale: $200M+ for road, rail, and industrial infrastructure
planSource: Regional Research Collaborative Strategy — NM Priority

title: Horizon City Infrastructure-Growth Mismatch
statement: Horizon City's population has doubled in 15 years but the city has no public water utility, limited wastewater capacity, and an underdeveloped road network. The commercial tax base is nearly nonexistent, creating a structural funding gap for essential services.
evidence: Census growth data; PSB water district reports; city budget analysis
estimatedScale: $50M-$80M for water, wastewater, and road infrastructure
planSource: Regional Plans Analysis — LGI Layer

title: Entrepreneurship and Startup Ecosystem Development
statement: El Paso ranks in the bottom quartile among mid-size metros for venture capital activity and startup formation. University technology transfer is underdeveloped. Las Cruces has emerging momentum through NMSU's Arrowhead Center but lacks scale.
evidence: Kauffman Foundation data; PitchBook VC tracking; UTEP Tech Transfer reports
estimatedScale: $25M-$50M in ecosystem development funding
planSource: CoEP Strategic Plan — Economic Mobility; UTEP Innovation Strategy

title: Semiconductor and Advanced Technology Workforce Gap
statement: Texas CHIPS Act implementation requires 10,000+ semiconductor technicians statewide by 2030. The Borderplex has no semiconductor fab but has the engineering talent pipeline (UTEP) and proximity to advanced manufacturing clusters (Juárez) to compete for supply chain roles.
evidence: CHIPS Act workforce projections; TX Higher Ed Coordinating Board reports
estimatedScale: $50M-$100M in training and lab infrastructure
planSource: Borderplex Funding Pipeline — Semiconductor Ecosystem

title: Heritage Tourism and Cultural Economy Underdevelopment
statement: San Elizario, the oldest community in Texas (1789), and the broader El Camino Real corridor have significant heritage tourism potential that remains undeveloped. Cultural economy could diversify revenue for small communities but lacks marketing, infrastructure, and preservation investment.
evidence: Texas Historical Commission reports; visitor statistics; community cultural plans
estimatedScale: $10M-$20M for preservation, marketing, and infrastructure
planSource: Crosswalk Analysis — Quality of Life Pillar

title: Aerospace and Space Commerce Workforce Readiness
statement: Spaceport America (150 miles north) and the growing commercial space sector create workforce demand that the region is not meeting. UTEP's Aerospace Center and NMSU's engineering programs need pipeline coordination with Blue Origin, SpaceX supply chain, and NASA WSTF.
evidence: Spaceport America activity reports; aerospace workforce demand projections
estimatedScale: $20M-$40M in workforce and research infrastructure
planSource: UTEP-NM Funding Partner Mapping — Aerospace Sector

title: Community Health Worker and Promotora Pipeline
statement: The Borderplex's Hispanic-majority population (82%) relies heavily on community health workers (promotoras) for health navigation, but the pipeline is informal, underfunded, and unscaled. Both cities identify human services access as a priority.
evidence: HRSA CHW data; DSHS promotora registry; border health needs assessments
estimatedScale: $15M-$30M for training, certification, and sustainable funding
planSource: CoEP Strategic Plan — Quality of Life; Las Cruces — Education & Community Services

title: Cross-Border Water Sharing and Binational Aquifer Management
statement: The Hueco and Mesilla Bolson aquifers serve both El Paso and Ciudad Juárez but are being depleted faster than recharge rates. No binding binational governance framework exists for shared aquifer management. Desalination and water reuse are technically feasible but unfunded at scale.
evidence: USGS aquifer monitoring; IBWC reports; El Paso Water Utilities long-range plan
estimatedScale: $500M+ for desalination, reuse, and aquifer management
planSource: Regional Research Collaborative Strategy — Binational Priority

title: Permitting and Licensing Process Inefficiency
statement: El Paso businesses report permitting timelines 2-3x longer than peer cities. The FY27-28 Strategic Plan specifically targets fast-track permitting, clear guidance, interdepartmental coordination, and predictable timelines as priorities.
evidence: FY27-28 Strategic Plan; business climate surveys; peer city benchmarking
estimatedScale: $5M-$10M for technology and process improvement
planSource: FY2027-FY2028 CoEP Strategic Plan — Economic Mobility Pillar

title: Quantum Technology and National Security Research Corridor
statement: New Mexico's national labs (Sandia, Los Alamos) are global leaders in quantum computing and sensing. The Borderplex region has no coordinated strategy to capture the quantum workforce, testing, or commercialization pipeline despite geographic proximity and UTEP/NMSU talent.
evidence: NSF Quantum Leap reports; Sandia quantum computing announcements; DOE roadmaps
estimatedScale: $100M+ in federal quantum research funding available nationally
planSource: UTEP-NM Funding Partner Mapping — Outlier Priority (NM)
